subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — Beginning of Grade (BOG) Test 1
instructions: Read each selection carefully. Then choose the best answer for each question. Some questions ask you to select more than one answer.

passages.title: The New Bike
passages.text:
Part 1

Sam wanted to ride a bike more than anything. His older sister, Lena, could ride down the whole block without falling. Sam still used training wheels. Every time he watched Lena zoom past, his cheeks turned warm.

"I will never learn," Sam said one morning. He kicked a small rock across the driveway.

Lena sat down next to him. "I fell a hundred times before I could ride," she said. "Falling is how you learn. Do you want me to help you?"

Sam nodded slowly. He was not sure he believed her, but he wanted to try.

Part 2

Lena took off Sam's training wheels with a wrench. Then she held the back of the seat. "I have you," she said. "Just push the pedals and look straight ahead."

Sam pushed. The bike wobbled. He pushed again, and the bike rolled forward. Lena jogged behind him, keeping her hand on the seat.

Then Sam felt something change. The seat felt light. He looked back and saw Lena standing far behind him, smiling and waving. She had let go, and he was riding all by himself!

Sam was so surprised that he forgot to steer. The bike tipped, and he landed softly in the grass. But he was laughing.

"You did it!" Lena shouted, running to him. "You rode by yourself!"

Sam stood up and brushed the grass off his knees. His cheeks were warm again, but this time it was from pride. "Let's go again," he said. "I want to ride all the way down the block."

passages.title: Animals That Glow
passages.text:
Part 1

Have you ever seen a tiny light blink in the dark on a summer night? That light may come from a firefly. Some animals can make their own light. This special light is called bioluminescence. It does not feel hot like a lamp. Instead, it is a cool light that the animal makes inside its body.

Fireflies are not the only animals that glow. Deep in the ocean, where sunlight cannot reach, many sea creatures make light too. The water there is dark and cold. Glowing helps these animals live in a world without sunlight.

Part 2

Why would an animal make light? Animals glow for different reasons. A firefly flashes its light to send a message to other fireflies. The flashes help fireflies find a mate in the dark.

Some fish use light in a clever way. The anglerfish has a glowing part that hangs in front of its mouth, like a tiny fishing pole. Smaller fish swim toward the light because they are curious. When they get close, the anglerfish catches them. The glowing light helps the anglerfish find food.

Other sea animals use light to stay safe. When a tiny shrimp is in danger, it can squirt out a cloud of glowing liquid. The bright cloud surprises the hungry animal chasing it. While the predator is confused, the shrimp swims away and hides.

Making light is a useful tool. It helps animals talk to one another, find a meal, and escape from danger. The next time you see a firefly blink, remember that it is sending a tiny message in the dark.

passages.title: George Washington Carver and the Peanut
passages.text:
Part 1

George Washington Carver was a scientist who loved plants. He was born long ago, when life was very hard for many people. As a child, George was so curious about growing things that neighbors called him "the plant doctor." He would visit sick plants and find ways to help them grow strong again.

When George grew up, he taught at a school in Alabama. There he saw a big problem. Many farmers in the South grew only cotton. Growing the same crop year after year took all the goodness out of the soil. The tired soil could no longer grow healthy cotton, and the farmers were poor and worried.

Part 2

George had an idea. He told the farmers to plant peanuts instead of cotton for a while. Peanuts put goodness back into the soil. The next year, the soil would be rich again, and the cotton would grow better.

The farmers tried his idea, and it worked. But soon there was a new problem. The farmers had grown so many peanuts that they could not sell them all. Boxes of peanuts sat unused.

George went to work in his laboratory. He studied the peanut and found hundreds of new ways to use it. He showed people how to make soap, glue, ink, and even a kind of milk from peanuts. Because of his work, peanuts became useful and valuable.

George Washington Carver helped farmers in two ways. He helped the soil become healthy, and he helped people find new uses for their crops. He spent his life solving problems with the plants he loved.

passages.title: The Garden in Spring
passages.text:
Part 1

The garden wakes when winter ends,
and soft green shoots push through.
The sleepy seeds I planted deep
have heard the springtime, too.

The robin hops from branch to branch
and sings a cheerful tune.
The sun climbs higher every day
and warms the earth by noon.

Part 2

I water rows of little plants
and pull the weeds away.
I watch them stretch and reach for light
a little more each day.

The peas climb up their wooden poles,
the carrots hide below.
With sun and rain and gentle care,
my garden starts to grow.

So I will wait and tend it well,
through every sunny hour.
For seeds that wait beneath the ground
will someday be a flower.

questions.questionText: At the beginning of the story, what does Sam want to do more than anything?
options: Win a race against Lena
Fix his sister's bike
Ride a bike like his sister
Plant a garden
questions.explanation: The first sentence says Sam wanted to ride a bike more than anything.

questions.questionText: According to the story, what does Lena do to Sam's bike before he rides it?
options: She puts air in the tires.
She adds a basket to the front.
She paints the bike a new color.
She takes off the training wheels.
questions.explanation: In Part 2, Lena took off Sam's training wheels with a wrench.

questions.questionText: Which word BEST describes Lena in this story?
options: Shy
Helpful
Selfish
Angry
questions.explanation: Lena offers to help Sam, holds his seat, and cheers for him, which shows she is helpful.

questions.questionText: Read this sentence from Part 1: "Every time he watched Lena zoom past, his cheeks turned warm." What does the word "zoom" mean here?
options: Turn around slowly
Fall down hard
Stop and rest
Move by quickly
questions.explanation: Lena could ride the whole block, so "zoom past" means she moved by quickly.

questions.questionText: What happens right AFTER Sam feels the seat become light?
options: He looks back and sees that Lena let go.
He decides he will never learn.
He puts the training wheels back on.
He kicks a rock across the driveway.
questions.explanation: After the seat felt light, Sam looked back and saw Lena standing far behind him because she had let go.

questions.questionText: At the end, the story says Sam's "cheeks were warm again, but this time it was from pride." Why are his cheeks warm for a different reason now?
options: He is angry because Lena let go too soon.
He is cold because he sat in the wet grass.
He is embarrassed because he fell in the grass.
He is proud because he rode the bike by himself.
questions.explanation: Earlier his cheeks turned warm from embarrassment, but now they are warm from pride because he rode by himself.

questions.questionText: What is the main lesson, or theme, of this story?
options: Bikes are safer with training wheels on them.
Older sisters always ride faster than younger brothers.
It is better to give up than to keep trying.
Trying and falling can help you learn something new.
questions.explanation: Lena says falling is how you learn, and Sam learns to ride after wobbling and tipping over, showing the theme that trying and falling help you learn.

questions.questionText: What does Sam want to do at the very end of the story?
options: Ride all the way down the block
Put the training wheels back on
Go inside and rest
Watch Lena ride instead
questions.explanation: At the end Sam says, "I want to ride all the way down the block."

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "bioluminescence" as it is used in Part 1?
options: A summer night when fireflies sleep
A cool light that an animal makes inside its body
A deep part of the ocean with no fish
A hot lamp that people turn on at night
questions.explanation: Part 1 says bioluminescence is a cool light that the animal makes inside its body.

questions.questionText: According to the text, why does a firefly flash its light?
options: To send a message to other fireflies
To help people see in the dark
To dry the water off its wings
To keep itself warm at night
questions.explanation: Part 2 says a firefly flashes its light to send a message to other fireflies.

questions.questionText: How does the anglerfish use its glowing light?
options: To scare away larger fish that chase it
To warm the cold ocean water around it
To light up the path back to its nest
To attract smaller fish so it can catch them
questions.explanation: The text says smaller fish swim toward the light, and when they get close, the anglerfish catches them.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this text?
options: Fireflies are the only animals that can glow at night.
Some animals make their own light, and it helps them in different ways.
The ocean is too dark and cold for any animal to live in.
People should catch fireflies to use as lamps.
questions.explanation: The whole text explains that some animals make light and how that light helps them, which is the main idea.

questions.questionText: What does a tiny shrimp do when it is in danger?
options: It swims toward a bigger, brighter light.
It hangs a glowing pole in front of its mouth.
It squirts out a cloud of glowing liquid.
It flashes its light to call for help.
questions.explanation: The text says when a shrimp is in danger, it can squirt out a cloud of glowing liquid to surprise the animal chasing it.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in the paragraphs of Part 2?
options: By comparing the ocean to a summer night
By showing what happens before and after sunset
By giving different reasons that animals glow
By telling the steps to catch a firefly in order
questions.explanation: Each paragraph in Part 2 gives a different reason animals glow: to send messages, to find food, and to stay safe.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the text BEST supports the idea that glowing helps animals stay safe?
options: "The glowing light helps the anglerfish find food."
"While the predator is confused, the shrimp swims away and hides."
"The flashes help fireflies find a mate in the dark."
"It does not feel hot like a lamp."
questions.explanation: The shrimp escaping while the predator is confused best shows how glowing keeps an animal safe.

questions.questionText: Select the two reasons the text gives for why animals make light.
options: To turn off the sunlight in the deep sea
To send messages, like the firefly does
To make the ocean water warmer
To find food, like the anglerfish does
questions.explanation: The text says animals glow to find food (the anglerfish) and to send messages (the firefly). It does not say glowing warms the water or turns off sunlight.

questions.questionText: What did neighbors call George Washington Carver when he was a child?
options: "The peanut seller"
"The cotton farmer"
"The plant doctor"
"The school teacher"
questions.explanation: Part 1 says neighbors called George "the plant doctor" because he was so curious about growing things.

questions.questionText: According to the text, why was the soil in the South tired and poor?
options: The farmers planted peanuts every year.
No one watered the fields in the summer.
Too much rain washed the soil away.
Farmers grew only cotton year after year.
questions.explanation: The text says growing the same crop, cotton, year after year took all the goodness out of the soil.

questions.questionText: In Part 2, the text says peanuts became "valuable." What does "valuable" mean?
options: Very hard to grow
Easy to throw away
Worth a lot and useful
Small and round
questions.explanation: After George found hundreds of uses for peanuts, they became worth a lot and useful, which is what "valuable" means.

questions.questionText: What did George tell the farmers to do to help the tired soil?
options: Stop farming and sell their land
Plant peanuts instead of cotton for a while
Add more cotton to the fields
Water the soil with peanut milk
questions.explanation: George told the farmers to plant peanuts instead of cotton because peanuts put goodness back into the soil.

questions.questionText: What is the connection between the farmers growing too many peanuts and George working in his laboratory?
options: The farmers worked in the laboratory with George.
The new problem led George to find new uses for peanuts.
George grew the extra peanuts in his laboratory.
George studied cotton instead of peanuts.
questions.explanation: Because the farmers grew more peanuts than they could sell, George went to his laboratory and found hundreds of new uses for them, showing cause and effect.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this text?
options: Farmers should always sell their crops in boxes.
Cotton is the most important crop in the South.
Peanuts are the easiest plants for children to grow.
George Washington Carver used his love of plants to solve problems for farmers.
questions.explanation: The text describes how George solved the soil problem and the unused-peanut problem, showing he used his love of plants to help farmers.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the text BEST supports the idea that George helped farmers in more than one way?
options: "He helped the soil become healthy, and he helped people find new uses for their crops."
"Boxes of peanuts sat unused."
"He was born long ago, when life was very hard for many people."
"When George grew up, he taught at a school in Alabama."
questions.explanation: This sentence directly states the two ways George helped farmers: healthy soil and new uses for crops.

questions.questionText: What is one product the text says people learned to make from peanuts?
options: Soap
Wood
Cotton
Snow
questions.explanation: The text says George showed people how to make soap, glue, ink, and even a kind of milk from peanuts.

questions.questionText: In the poem, what wakes up when winter ends?
options: The garden
The snow
The moon
The wind
questions.explanation: The first line says, "The garden wakes when winter ends."

questions.questionText: According to the poem, what does the speaker do to take care of the little plants?
options: Carries them inside the house
Waters them and pulls the weeds
Cuts them down and dries them
Covers them with snow
questions.explanation: In Part 2 the speaker says, "I water rows of little plants / and pull the weeds away."

questions.questionText: Read these lines: "The peas climb up their wooden poles, / the carrots hide below." What does it mean that the carrots "hide below"?
options: The carrots climb the wooden poles too.
The carrots run away from the garden.
The carrots grow under the ground.
The carrots are scared of the peas.
questions.explanation: Carrots grow under the ground, so saying they "hide below" means they grow below the soil.

questions.questionText: Read this line from the poem: "The sleepy seeds I planted deep / have heard the springtime, too." What does the poet mean by calling the seeds "sleepy"?
options: The seeds were planted by a sleeping person.
The seeds are too tired to ever grow.
The seeds have been resting and are now starting to grow.
The seeds make a soft sound at night.
questions.explanation: Calling the seeds "sleepy" and saying they "heard the springtime" shows the seeds were resting and are now waking up to grow.

questions.questionText: What is the main message of the poem?
options: With care and patience, a garden will grow over time.
Winter is the best season for planting seeds.
Robins keep the garden from growing.
Gardens grow best when no one takes care of them.
questions.explanation: The speaker waters, weeds, and waits, and the poem ends saying the seeds will someday be a flower, showing that care and patience help a garden grow.

questions.questionText: How is the poem organized?
options: It follows the garden as it grows from seeds to plants.
It lists the names of every flower in the world.
It tells about one single rainy day.
It compares two different gardens.
questions.explanation: The poem moves from the garden waking and seeds sprouting to plants growing and finally becoming a flower, following the garden as it grows.

questions.questionText: Select the two things the poem says the speaker watches happen in the garden.
options: The garden fills with snow.
The plants stretch and reach for light.
The wooden poles grow taller.
The robin hops and sings.
questions.explanation: The poem says the robin hops from branch to branch and sings, and that the speaker watches the plants stretch and reach for light. The garden does not fill with snow, and the poles do not grow.

questions.questionText: Use both parts of this text to answer this question. How are Part 1 and Part 2 of the poem alike?
options: Both parts tell about a robin building a nest.
Both parts show the garden coming to life in spring.
Both parts list reasons not to plant a garden.
Both parts describe a cold and snowy winter day.
questions.explanation: Part 1 shows the garden waking and seeds sprouting, and Part 2 shows the plants growing with care; both parts show the garden coming to life in spring.
